note_1_2_0.js.title: Apply ALL THREE Factors of Fick's Law
note_1_2_0.js.text: Examiners deduct marks if you mention only one or two of Fick's Law factors (surface area, concentration gradient, thickness). Always state explicitly: rate ∝ (SA × ΔC) / thickness. This is critical for explaining any gas exchange scenario.

note_1_2_0.js.title: Link Theory to Real Biology
note_1_2_0.js.text: Don't just recite Fick's Law mathematically. Show how it explains why organisms need adaptations: single-celled organisms don't need lungs (small size = large SA:V), but large organisms do.

note_1_2_1.js.title: TWO Layers of Squamous Epithelium, Not One
note_1_2_1.js.text: A common error: saying 'the alveolar wall is one cell thick.' Correct: the diffusion distance spans TWO one-cell-thick layers: the alveolar epithelium AND the capillary endothelium. Both must be thin.

note_1_2_1.js.title: Gradient Maintenance Requires BOTH Ventilation and Blood Flow
note_1_2_1.js.text: Examiners expect you to explain HOW the steep gradient is maintained: ventilation removes CO₂-rich air AND brings fresh O₂; blood flow removes oxygenated blood AND brings deoxygenated blood. Only mentioning one is incomplete.

note_1_2_2.js.title: Connect CF to Fick's Law Directly
note_1_2_2.js.text: Don't just describe CF symptoms. Show how thick mucus reduces diffusion rate: (1) blocks airflow → reduced SA, (2) sits on alveolar walls → increased diffusion distance. This demonstrates mastery of the principle.

note_1_2_2.js.title: Bacterial Infection is Secondary, But Damaging
note_1_2_2.js.text: The infection itself further damages lung tissue, creating a vicious cycle. Exam questions often ask 'Why do CF patients eventually have severe lung damage?' – the answer extends beyond thick mucus to infection-driven tissue destruction.

note_1_2_3.js.title: Cholesterol Restricts MOVEMENT, Not Permeability
note_1_2_3.js.text: Critical misconception: 'Cholesterol makes the membrane less permeable.' WRONG. Cholesterol restricts the MOVEMENT/FLUIDITY of phospholipids but does NOT block passage of molecules. It stabilizes the membrane at higher temperatures.

note_1_2_3.js.title: Integral vs Peripheral Proteins Have Different Functions
note_1_2_3.js.text: Integral proteins span the bilayer and act as channels/carriers. Peripheral proteins sit on the surface and often provide structural support. Examiners expect you to distinguish these roles when describing membrane proteins.

note_1_2_4.js.title: Simple Diffusion: Only Non-Polar Molecules
note_1_2_4.js.text: Common error: saying glucose uses simple diffusion. FALSE. Only small, non-polar molecules (O₂, CO₂, lipids) cross directly. Glucose is polar and MUST use facilitated diffusion via protein carriers.

note_1_2_4.js.title: Osmosis Definition Must Reference Water Potential
note_1_2_4.js.text: Saying 'water moves to high concentration' is imprecise. Correct: water moves from higher (less negative) water potential to lower (more negative) water potential across a partially permeable membrane. The terminology matters in exams.

note_1_2_5.js.title: Active Transport REQUIRES ATP – State This First
note_1_2_5.js.text: This is the defining feature. Always explicitly state: active transport moves substances AGAINST their gradient and requires ATP from respiration. Missing 'requires ATP' loses critical marks.

note_1_2_5.js.title: Both Endo- and Exocytosis Are Active: They Require ATP
note_1_2_5.js.text: Students sometimes forget that bulk transport isn't 'free.' Endocytosis: membrane engulfs material (ATP-driven). Exocytosis: vesicles fuse and expel contents (ATP-driven). Both require energy; mention this explicitly.

note_1_2_6.js.title: Water Moves to LOWER (More Negative) Potential, Not Higher Concentration
note_1_2_6.js.text: Exam pitfall: stating 'water moves toward salt' is sloppy. Be precise: water moves from Ψ ≈ 0 (pure) toward Ψ < 0 (salty). Using 'water potential' language demonstrates A-Level understanding.

note_1_2_6.js.title: Plasmolysis is Reversible in Living Plant Cells
note_1_2_6.js.text: When examiners ask about plasmolysis, clarify: in concentrated solution, the protoplasm shrinks away from the cell wall. If returned to dilute solution, the cell can recover (reversible plasmolysis). This shows understanding of osmotic mechanisms.

note_1_2_7.js.title: Always Mention the R Group Varies – This Creates Diversity
note_1_2_7.js.text: When describing amino acid structure, don't just list the four groups. Emphasize: the R group differs between the ~20 amino acids, and THIS is what gives proteins their unique properties and functions.

note_1_2_7.js.title: Peptide Bond Forms Between Specific Groups
note_1_2_7.js.text: State precisely: carboxyl group (-COOH) of ONE amino acid reacts with amine group (-NH₂) of ANOTHER via condensation, releasing H₂O and forming a C-N covalent bond. Vague descriptions lose marks.

note_1_2_8.js.title: Tertiary Structure: Know All FOUR Bond Types
note_1_2_8.js.text: Examiners often ask, 'List the bonds holding tertiary structure.' Must name all four: hydrogen bonds, van der Waals forces, ionic bonds, AND disulfide bridges (cvalent S-S). Missing any costs marks. Disulfide bridges are the STRONGEST.

note_1_2_8.js.title: Do Not Confuse H-Bonds at Secondary and Tertiary Levels
note_1_2_8.js.text: Secondary: H-bonds form in the backbone (C=O to N-H in the chain). Tertiary: H-bonds form in R-groups. This distinction is critical; vague language ('H-bonds throughout') suggests misunderstanding.

note_1_2_9.js.title: Use 'Induced Fit Model,' Not 'Lock and Key'
note_1_2_9.js.text: Modern syllabi expect the induced fit model. State: the active site is not rigid; it molds around the substrate as it binds, straining the substrate bonds. Simply writing 'lock and key' suggests outdated knowledge.

note_1_2_9.js.title: Enzymes Lower Activation Energy Without Being Consumed
note_1_2_9.js.text: Examiners test whether you understand: enzymes speed reactions by reducing the energy needed (lowering activation energy) and are NOT used up—they can catalyse many cycles. Both parts are exam-essential.
